findings.title: Missing Email Validation in Contact Form
findings.description: The contact form in the Netlify function only checks if email exists and is non-empty using !email?.trim(), but does not validate the email format. An attacker could submit malformed email addresses that could be used in email header injection attacks or cause issues with the email delivery system. The email is used directly in the replyTo field and subject line without proper validation.

findings.title: Potential Email Header Injection in Contact Form
findings.description: The contact form uses user-provided name and email directly in email headers (subject line and from field) without sanitizing newline characters. An attacker could inject additional email headers by including \r\n sequences in the name or email field, potentially adding BCC recipients, modifying headers, or conducting email spam through the server.

findings.title: XSS Vulnerability in Contact Form HTML Email
findings.description: User-provided input (name, email, message) is directly interpolated into HTML email content without proper encoding. While this affects email clients rather than the web application, attackers could inject malicious HTML/JavaScript that would execute in the email reader if it supports JavaScript or could be used for phishing with crafted HTML content.
findings.remediation:
Use HTML encoding for user input in the email HTML body: ```typescript const escapeHtml = (str: string) => str.replace(/&/g, '&').replace(//g, '>').replace(/"/g, '"').replace(/'/g, '''); html: `

From: ${escapeHtml(name.trim())} <${escapeHtml(email.trim())}>

${escapeHtml(message.trim()).replace(/\n/g, '
')}

` ```

findings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Contact Form Endpoint
findings.description: The Netlify function endpoint for sending contact emails has no rate limiting. An attacker could abuse this to send a large volume of emails through the server, potentially causing email service quotas to be exceeded, the SMTP account to be flagged for spam, or the email service to be suspended.
findings.remediation:
Implement rate limiting at the Netlify level using their built-in features or add IP-based rate limiting in the function:

1. Use Netlify's built-in rate limiting in netlify.toml:
```toml
[[edge_functions]]
path = "/.netlify/functions/send-contact-email"
function = "rate-limit"
```

2. Or implement application-level rate limiting with a store like Redis or use a simple in-memory solution for basic protection.

findings.title: Missing Security Headers in index.html
findings.description: The main HTML page does not include security headers such as Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, or Referrer-Policy. While Netlify or the hosting provider may add some headers, not having them explicitly configured means relying on defaults that may not be secure.
findings.remediation:
Add security headers via Netlify's _headers file or netlify.toml:

```toml
[[headers]]
  for = "/*"
  [headers.values]
    X-Frame-Options = "DENY"
    X-Content-Type-Options = "nosniff"
    Referrer-Policy = "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
    Content-Security-Policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com; img-src 'self' data:;"
```

findings.title: Personal Contact Information Exposed in Source Code
findings.description: Personal identifiable information including phone number (+31687269021) and personal email address (abdulrahmanabdulqawi76@gmail.com) are hardcoded in the source code. While this is a portfolio site and exposure may be intentional, this information is easily scraped and could be used for spam, phishing, or social engineering attacks.
findings.remediation:
If protection is desired, consider:
1. Using contact forms exclusively without exposing direct contact info
2. Using obfuscation techniques for displayed contact info
3. Implementing CAPTCHA on the contact form to prevent automated scraping

findings.title: External Script Loading Without Subresource Integrity
findings.description: The stitch design template HTML files load external scripts from cdn.tailwindcss.com without Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes. If the CDN is compromised, malicious code could be injected. While these appear to be design mockups and not production code, this pattern should not be followed in production.
findings.remediation:
Add SRI hashes to external script includes:

```html
<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com" integrity="sha384-..." crossorigin="anonymous"></script>
```

Or better yet, use local Tailwind builds instead of CDN loading in production.

findings.title: Error Details Exposed in API Response
findings.description: The send-contact-email function logs the full error to console but returns a generic error message to the client. While this is generally good practice, the missing environment variable hint exposes internal configuration details that could help attackers understand the system setup.

findings.title: localStorage Used for User Preferences Without Encryption
findings.description: User preferences (theme, language, layout) are stored in localStorage without any protection. While these are non-sensitive settings, any XSS vulnerability would allow attackers to read and modify these values. This is noted for awareness rather than as a significant vulnerability given the data stored.
findings.remediation: This is acceptable for non-sensitive user preferences. Ensure no sensitive data is ever stored in localStorage. If needed, consider using sessionStorage for temporary settings or implementing CSRF tokens for any state-changing operations.

findings.title: Form Without CSRF Protection
findings.description: The contact forms (both in Contact.tsx and ConsolePromptView.tsx) submit data to external endpoints without CSRF tokens. The Netlify form uses the built-in Netlify Forms handler which has its own protection, but the console view sends directly to the serverless function which has no CSRF protection.
findings.remediation:
While the risk is low for a contact form (no state-changing operations for the user), consider adding CSRF protection:

1. Generate a CSRF token server-side and include in the page
2. Validate the token on form submission
3. Or use the SameSite cookie attribute and verify Origin/Referer headers

findings.title: Unpinned Dependency Versions
findings.description: Several dependencies in package.json use caret (^) version ranges which allow automatic minor/patch updates. While generally safe, this could introduce vulnerabilities or breaking changes if a dependency is compromised or has a security issue in a newer version that auto-updates.
findings.remediation:
Consider using exact versions or lock files:
1. Use package-lock.json (already present) and commit it
2. Or use exact versions: "react": "18.3.1" instead of "^18.3.1"
3. Regularly audit dependencies with `npm audit`
4. Consider using tools like Dependabot or Snyk for vulnerability monitoring

findings.title: Open External Links Without noopener
findings.description: Most external links in the codebase properly use rel="noopener noreferrer", which is good practice. However, the pattern is inconsistent in some places where target="_blank" is used conditionally. While modern browsers have mitigated the tabnabbing vulnerability, maintaining consistent security practices is recommended.
findings.remediation:
Ensure all external links consistently use both noopener and noreferrer:

```tsx
<a
  href={link.url}
  target="_blank"
  rel="noopener noreferrer"
>
```

topRisks: Email header injection vulnerability in contact form allows attackers to potentially inject additional headers
Missing input validation on email format could allow malformed submissions
XSS in email HTML content could be exploited for phishing in email clients
summary.recommendation: This portfolio website has a generally good security posture with no critical or high-severity vulnerabilities. The primary concerns are in the contact form serverless function where user input is not fully sanitized before being used in email headers and HTML content. Priority actions: 1) Sanitize all user input in the contact form to prevent email header injection, 2) Add HTML encoding for email content, 3) Implement rate limiting on the contact endpoint, 4) Add security headers via Netlify configuration. The codebase follows good practices like using rel="noopener noreferrer" for external links and storing only non-sensitive data in localStorage.
